answer:
1. Definition & Core Concept

Scaling Playwright E2E for 1 M+ users means building an E2E test infrastructure that validates critical user journeys across browsers, devices, and network conditions representative of a global user base. At scale, E2E tests must cover: multi-tenant scenarios, concurrent user interactions (two managers approving simultaneously), accessibility compliance across locales, and performance under realistic network conditions (3G, high latency).

2. Concrete Use-Case from Manoj's Projects

In ContactsHub, Manoj wrote 60+ Playwright E2E tests covering CRUD, bulk CSV upload, Canvas API image crop, and PWA offline mode. Scaling this to 1 M+ users, Manoj would add: (1) Parallel browser matrix: Chromium, Firefox, WebKit running concurrently via Playwright's projects config. (2) Network throttling: tests with page.route('**/*', route => route.continue({ delay: 200 })) simulating 3G. (3) Multi-tenant tests: two browser contexts (manager A and employee B) interacting simultaneously — verifying real-time Socket.io updates. (4) Visual regression: screenshot comparison for the leave calendar across viewport sizes (mobile, tablet, desktop).

3. Visual SVG Diagram

4. Code Example

// playwright.config.ts — scaled configuration
import { defineConfig, devices } from '@playwright/test';

export default defineConfig({
  testDir: './e2e',
  fullyParallel: true,
  workers: process.env.CI ? 4 : undefined,
  retries: process.env.CI ? 2 : 0,
  reporter: [['html'], ['json', { outputFile: 'test-results.json' }]],

  projects: [
    { name: 'chromium', use: { ...devices['Desktop Chrome'] } },
    { name: 'firefox', use: { ...devices['Desktop Firefox'] } },
    { name: 'webkit', use: { ...devices['Desktop Safari'] } },
    { name: 'mobile', use: { ...devices['iPhone 14'] } },
  ],

  use: {
    baseURL: 'http://localhost:3000',
    screenshot: 'only-on-failure',
    trace: 'on-first-retry',
  },
});

// Multi-tenant test: manager approves while employee watches
import { test, expect } from '@playwright/test';

test('real-time approval notification', async ({ browser }) => {
  // Employee context
  const employeeCtx = await browser.newContext();
  const employeePage = await employeeCtx.newPage();
  await employeePage.goto('/login');
  await employeePage.fill('[name=email]', 'employee@hr.com');
  await employeePage.fill('[name=password]', 'test123');
  await employeePage.click('button[type=submit]');
  await employeePage.goto('/my-leaves');

  // Manager context
  const managerCtx = await browser.newContext();
  const managerPage = await managerCtx.newPage();
  await managerPage.goto('/login');
  await managerPage.fill('[name=email]', 'manager@hr.com');
  await managerPage.fill('[name=password]', 'test123');
  await managerPage.click('button[type=submit]');
  await managerPage.goto('/approvals');

  // Manager approves
  await managerPage.click('[data-testid="approve-btn-0"]');
  await expect(managerPage.locator('.toast')).toContainText('Approved');

  // Employee sees real-time update (Socket.io)
  await expect(employeePage.locator('[data-testid="leave-status-0"]'))
    .toContainText('Approved', { timeout: 5000 });

  await employeeCtx.close();
  await managerCtx.close();
});

// Network throttle test for PWA
test('works offline after caching', async ({ page }) => {
  await page.goto('/contacts');
  await page.waitForLoadState('networkidle');

  // Go offline
  await page.context().setOffline(true);
  await page.reload();

  // PWA shows cached contacts
  await expect(page.locator('.contact-card')).toHaveCount.greaterThan(0);
});

5. Debugging Narrative from Manoj's Experience

ContactsHub's E2E test for bulk CSV upload was flaky — passing 80% of the time. Manoj added Playwright traces (trace: 'on-first-retry') and reviewed the trace viewer. The issue: the test clicked "Upload" before the file input's change event had fully processed (race condition between file selection and form submission). Fix: await page.waitForSelector('[data-testid="file-ready"]') — a data attribute set by React after processing the file. Flakiness dropped to 0%. For the multi-tenant test, Manoj initially used a single browser context and toggled between tabs — this caused state leakage. Using separate browser.newContext() for each user provided true isolation.

6. Pitfall & Mitigation Table

PitfallImpact at ScaleMitigation
Flaky tests from race conditionsFalse failures block CI pipelineUse data-testid + waitForSelector; retries: 2 in CI
Single browser onlyBugs in Firefox/Safari go undetectedMulti-browser projects config (Chromium + Firefox + WebKit)
No network condition testingApp breaks on slow connectionsTest with setOffline and route delay simulation
Shared browser context for multi-userCookie/state leakage between usersSeparate browser.newContext() per user
Too many E2E testsCI takes 30+ minutesShard across workers; keep E2E to critical paths only

7. Validation Strategy

  • Flakiness tracking: CI tracks retry rate per test; quarantine tests with > 5% flake rate for investigation.
  • Browser coverage: 100% of E2E tests run on Chromium + Firefox + WebKit; report per-browser pass rate.
  • CI time budget: E2E suite < 5 minutes with sharding (4 workers × 3 browsers).
  • Trace on failure: Every failed test has a Playwright trace for debugging; no "works on my machine" excuses.

1. Definition & Core Concept

Playwright E2E performance bottlenecks appear as test suites grow: slow test execution from sequential runs, browser startup overhead per test, network waiting for API responses, screenshot comparison costs, and CI resource contention. At 60+ tests, a serial E2E suite can take 30+ minutes — blocking deployments and destroying developer confidence in CI.

2. Concrete Use-Case from Manoj's Projects

In ContactsHub's 60+ Playwright tests, the suite initially took 25 minutes in CI. Manoj profiled and found: (1) Browser startup: each test launched a new browser (3s each × 60 = 180s). Fix: reuse browser instance, use test.describe.configure({ mode: 'parallel' }). (2) API waiting: tests used page.waitForTimeout(2000) for API responses. Fix: replaced with page.waitForResponse(url) — waits only as long as needed. (3) No sharding: all 60 tests on one CI worker. Fix: --shard=1/4 across 4 parallel CI jobs. (4) Screenshot storage: 200 screenshots per run filling CI cache. Fix: screenshots only on failure (screenshot: 'only-on-failure').

3. Visual SVG Diagram

4. Code Example

// Optimized Playwright test — no arbitrary waits
import { test, expect } from '@playwright/test';

test('submit leave and see confirmation', async ({ page }) => {
  await page.goto('/leaves/new');

  // Fill form
  await page.fill('[name=startDate]', '2025-03-10');
  await page.fill('[name=endDate]', '2025-03-12');
  await page.selectOption('[name=leaveType]', 'annual');

  // Smart wait: intercept API response instead of waitForTimeout
  const responsePromise = page.waitForResponse(
    resp => resp.url().includes('/api/leaves') && resp.status() === 201
  );
  await page.click('[data-testid="submit-btn"]');
  const response = await responsePromise;
  const body = await response.json();
  expect(body.status).toBe('pending');

  // Verify UI updated
  await expect(page.locator('.success-toast')).toBeVisible();
});

// CI sharding — GitHub Actions
// .github/workflows/e2e.yml
// jobs:
//   test:
//     strategy:
//       matrix:
//         shard: [1, 2, 3, 4]
//     steps:
//       - run: npx playwright test --shard=${{ matrix.shard }}/4

// Slowest test identification
// npx playwright test --reporter=json | jq '.suites[].specs[] | {title: .title, duration: .tests[0].results[0].duration}' | sort_by(.duration)

5. Debugging Narrative from Manoj's Experience

ContactsHub's CI E2E suite took 25 minutes, blocking PRs. Manoj ran --reporter=json and parsed the output to find the 5 slowest tests — all had waitForTimeout(3000) calls. Replacing these with waitForResponse or waitForSelector saved 2-5 seconds per test (total: 3 minutes). Enabling fullyParallel: true with 4 workers cut the remaining 22 minutes to 6 minutes. Finally, CI sharding across 4 GitHub Actions runners brought the total to 1.5 minutes. The key insight: never use waitForTimeout — it's always either too short (flaky) or too long (slow).

6. Pitfall & Mitigation Table

PitfallImpact at ScaleMitigation
waitForTimeout() instead of smart waitsTests are both slow and flakyUse waitForResponse, waitForSelector, toBeVisible
Serial test execution in CI60 tests × 30s = 30 minutesfullyParallel + CI sharding
All screenshots storedCI cache bloat (200+ images per run)screenshot: 'only-on-failure'
No trace on failureCan't debug CI failures locallytrace: 'on-first-retry' captures full action replay
Not identifying slowest testsOptimization effort on wrong testsJSON reporter → parse slowest tests → optimize first

7. Validation Strategy

  • CI time budget: E2E suite < 3 minutes with sharding; alert if exceeded.
  • Zero waitForTimeout: ESLint rule bans waitForTimeout; PR review catches violations.
  • Flake rate: Track retries per test; quarantine any test with > 3% flake rate.
  • Slowest test review: Weekly review of top-5 slowest tests; optimize or split.

1. Definition & Core Concept

Test Data Factories deep dive covers the patterns for generating realistic, consistent test data: factory functions (create full objects with sensible defaults), builders (fluent API for complex construction), fixtures (pre-defined datasets for specific scenarios), and seeders (populate databases for integration tests). Trade-offs: factories are flexible but require maintenance; fixtures are simple but rigid; seeders create realistic data but slow down test setup. Failure modes: test data that doesn't represent production (uniform distributions, missing edge cases), and factories that drift from the actual schema.

2. Concrete Use-Case from Manoj's Projects

In the HR Leave Management System, Manoj built a factory library: createMockLeave() generated leaves with realistic defaults (weekday dates, valid leave types, proper status transitions). createMockApprovalChain() generated a leave with 3 approval steps at various stages. seedLeaveCalendar(month) populated a test database with 100 leaves distributed across a month with realistic patterns (more leaves on Mondays, none on holidays). Trade-off: the factory auto-generated UUIDs, which meant tests couldn't hardcode expected IDs — Manoj added an optional id override for deterministic tests. Failure mode: the factory generated startDate as a fixed date, causing tests to break when that date became "in the past" — fixed by using relative dates (addDays(new Date(), 7)).

3. Visual SVG Diagram

4. Code Example

// Test Data Factory for HR Leave System
// src/test/factories/leave.factory.ts

import { v4 as uuid } from 'uuid';

function addDays(date: Date, days: number): Date {
  const d = new Date(date);
  d.setDate(d.getDate() + days);
  return d;
}

function nextWeekday(): Date {
  const d = addDays(new Date(), 7);
  while ([0, 6].includes(d.getDay())) d.setDate(d.getDate() + 1);
  return d;
}

export function createMockLeave(overrides: Partial = {}): Leave {
  const start = nextWeekday(); // Always a future weekday
  return {
    id: uuid(),
    userId: uuid(),
    startDate: start,
    endDate: addDays(start, 2),
    days: 3,
    status: 'pending',
    leaveType: 'annual',
    version: 1,
    createdAt: new Date(),
    ...overrides,
  };
}

// Builder pattern for complex scenarios
class LeaveBuilder {
  private leave: Leave;

  constructor() {
    this.leave = createMockLeave();
  }

  approved() {
    this.leave.status = 'approved';
    return this;
  }

  forUser(userId: string) {
    this.leave.userId = userId;
    return this;
  }

  withDays(days: number) {
    this.leave.days = days;
    this.leave.endDate = addDays(this.leave.startDate, days);
    return this;
  }

  build(): Leave {
    return { ...this.leave };
  }
}

export const LeaveFactory = {
  create: (overrides?: Partial) => createMockLeave(overrides),
  builder: () => new LeaveBuilder(),
  approved: (overrides?: Partial) =>
    createMockLeave({ status: 'approved', ...overrides }),
  pending: (overrides?: Partial) =>
    createMockLeave({ status: 'pending', ...overrides }),
};

// Usage in tests — clean and expressive
const leave = LeaveFactory.builder()
  .forUser('user-1')
  .withDays(5)
  .approved()
  .build();

const pendingLeave = LeaveFactory.pending({ userId: 'user-2' });

5. Debugging Narrative from Manoj's Experience

On March 11, 2025, 15 tests suddenly failed in CI with "Cannot submit leave with past start date." The factory had hardcoded startDate: new Date('2025-03-10') — valid when written on March 5th, but now in the past. Manoj refactored the factory to use nextWeekday() which always returns a future weekday. This "time bomb" pattern is common in test factories — any absolute date becomes invalid eventually. The fix also handled weekends: the factory skips Saturday/Sunday to avoid triggering the weekend validation rule. After this incident, Manoj added a CI lint that grepped for hardcoded dates in test factories: grep -r "new Date('20" src/test/.

6. Pitfall & Mitigation Table

PitfallImpact at ScaleMitigation
Hardcoded dates in factoriesTests break when date passesUse relative dates (addDays(new Date(), 7))
Factory doesn't match schemaTests pass with wrong data shapeTypeScript types from Prisma schema enforce factory shape
No override supportEach test creates full objects from scratchSpread pattern: { ...defaults, ...overrides }
Random data without seedNon-deterministic tests, hard to reproduceUse faker with fixed seed for reproducible randomness
Factory bloat100+ factories hard to maintainOne factory per model; builder for complex scenarios

7. Validation Strategy

  • No hardcoded dates: CI lint rejects any hardcoded date in test/factory files.
  • Type check: Factory return type matches Prisma model type; compile error if schema changes.
  • Coverage: Every model has a factory; no raw object literals in test files.
  • Edge case factories: Factories for boundary conditions (0 days, max days, exactly remaining balance).

1. Definition & Core Concept

Scaling Test Data Factories for 1 M+ users means generating test data that represents production-like volumes and distributions: realistic user demographics, skewed data distributions (80% of leaves in summer months), edge cases at scale (10k concurrent users, bulk operations), and cross-tenant data isolation. At scale, a factory that generates 1 leave at a time is insufficient — you need bulk generation with controlled distributions.

2. Concrete Use-Case from Manoj's Projects

Scaling the HR Leave Management System's test data, Manoj would build: (1) Bulk factory: seedLeaves(100000, { distribution: 'production' }) generating 100k leaves with realistic patterns — 60% annual, 20% sick, 10% personal, 10% other; peak in December. (2) Tenant isolation: each test gets a unique departmentId prefix so parallel tests don't collide. (3) Faker with seed: faker.seed(42) for reproducible random data across CI runs. (4) Performance seeder: a Prisma $executeRaw with a bulk INSERT that seeds 100k rows in 5 seconds (vs 100k individual create() calls taking 10 minutes).

3. Visual SVG Diagram

4. Code Example

// Bulk test data factory for HR Leave System
import { faker } from '@faker-js/faker';

faker.seed(42); // Reproducible across CI runs

const LEAVE_TYPE_DISTRIBUTION = [
  ...Array(60).fill('annual'),
  ...Array(20).fill('sick'),
  ...Array(10).fill('personal'),
  ...Array(10).fill('other'),
];

export async function seedLeaves(
  count: number,
  prisma: PrismaClient,
  options: { departmentId?: string } = {}
) {
  const deptId = options.departmentId || faker.string.uuid();

  // Create users first
  const users = Array.from({ length: Math.min(count / 10, 100) }, () => ({
    id: faker.string.uuid(),
    name: faker.person.fullName(),
    email: faker.internet.email(),
    role: 'employee',
    departmentId: deptId,
  }));
  await prisma.user.createMany({ data: users });

  // Generate leaves with realistic distribution
  const leaves = Array.from({ length: count }, () => {
    const user = faker.helpers.arrayElement(users);
    const start = faker.date.future({ years: 1 });
    // Skip weekends
    while ([0, 6].includes(start.getDay())) start.setDate(start.getDate() + 1);
    const days = faker.number.int({ min: 1, max: 5 });

    return {
      id: faker.string.uuid(),
      userId: user.id,
      startDate: start,
      endDate: new Date(start.getTime() + days * 86400000),
      days,
      status: faker.helpers.weightedArrayElement([
        { value: 'pending', weight: 30 },
        { value: 'approved', weight: 50 },
        { value: 'rejected', weight: 20 },
      ]),
      leaveType: faker.helpers.arrayElement(LEAVE_TYPE_DISTRIBUTION),
      version: 1,
      createdAt: faker.date.recent({ days: 365 }),
    };
  });

  // Bulk insert in batches of 5000
  for (let i = 0; i < leaves.length; i += 5000) {
    await prisma.leave.createMany({
      data: leaves.slice(i, i + 5000),
    });
  }

  return { users, leaveCount: leaves.length, departmentId: deptId };
}

// Usage in performance integration test
describe('Leave API at scale', () => {
  let deptId: string;

  beforeAll(async () => {
    const result = await seedLeaves(100000, prisma);
    deptId = result.departmentId;
  }, 60000);

  it('paginates 100k leaves within 200ms', async () => {
    const start = performance.now();
    const response = await app.inject({
      method: 'GET',
      url: `/api/leaves?limit=50&departmentId=${deptId}`,
    });
    expect(performance.now() - start).toBeLessThan(200);
    expect(JSON.parse(response.body).length).toBe(50);
  });
});

5. Debugging Narrative from Manoj's Experience

Manoj's performance integration tests were seeded with 100 leaves — all with status: 'pending'. The tests passed, but production had 80% approved leaves. When the partial index (WHERE status = 'pending') was deployed, it worked perfectly for the 20% pending leaves in production but the test's 100% pending dataset didn't exercise the index correctly (the planner chose a seq scan because all rows matched). Manoj updated the seeder to use weighted distributions matching production ratios: 30% pending, 50% approved, 20% rejected. This realistic data revealed that the dashboard query's pagination needed a composite index, which the uniform test data had masked.

6. Pitfall & Mitigation Table

PitfallImpact at ScaleMitigation
Uniform data distributionQuery plans differ from productionWeighted distributions matching production ratios
Individual CREATE callsSeeding 100k takes 10+ minutescreateMany / bulk INSERT in batches
Random without seedNon-reproducible test failuresfaker.seed(42) for deterministic randomness
No tenant isolationParallel tests corrupt each other's dataUnique departmentId per test suite
Test data not cleaned upDB grows across test runs, CI disk fillsTruncate tables in afterAll or use Docker container

7. Validation Strategy

  • Distribution check: Assert seeded data matches target ratios (50% approved, 30% pending, 20% rejected).
  • Seed time: Seeding 100k rows must complete in < 30s; alert if slower.
  • Reproducibility: Same faker seed produces identical data across runs; verify with checksum.
  • Isolation: Run 4 test suites in parallel; verify zero cross-contamination via department prefix.

1. Definition & Core Concept

Scaling Contract Tests for 1 M+ users means ensuring that the API contracts between frontend and backend (and between microservices) remain stable as the system scales horizontally across multiple teams and services. Contract tests (using tools like Pact) define the expected request/response shapes that consumers expect from providers, catching breaking changes before deployment without requiring a running instance of every service.

2. Concrete Use-Case from Manoj's Projects

In the HR Leave Management System, the Angular 18 frontend consumed 12+ Fastify API endpoints. Without contract tests, a backend change (renaming startDate to start_date) would only be caught by E2E tests — which were slow and ran last in CI. Manoj would implement Pact contract tests: the Angular consumer defines expectations ("GET /api/leaves returns objects with startDate, endDate, status"), and the Fastify provider verifies it can fulfill those expectations. At scale with 1 M+ users and potentially 10+ frontend clients (web, mobile, admin panel), contract tests ensure API backward compatibility — no client breaks when the backend deploys.

3. Visual SVG Diagram

4. Code Example

// Consumer contract test (Angular side with Pact)
import { PactV3, MatchersV3 } from '@pact-foundation/pact';

const provider = new PactV3({
  consumer: 'HRDashboardAngular',
  provider: 'HRLeaveAPI',
});

describe('Leave API Contract', () => {
  it('returns pending leaves with expected shape', async () => {
    await provider
      .given('there are pending leaves')
      .uponReceiving('a request for pending leaves')
      .withRequest({
        method: 'GET',
        path: '/api/leaves',
        query: { status: 'pending' },
        headers: { Authorization: MatchersV3.string('Bearer token') },
      })
      .willRespondWith({
        status: 200,
        headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/json' },
        body: MatchersV3.eachLike({
          id: MatchersV3.uuid(),
          startDate: MatchersV3.iso8601DateTime(),
          endDate: MatchersV3.iso8601DateTime(),
          status: MatchersV3.string('pending'),
          days: MatchersV3.integer(3),
          user: {
            name: MatchersV3.string('Manoj'),
          },
        }),
      })
      .executeTest(async (mockServer) => {
        const response = await fetch(
          `${mockServer.url}/api/leaves?status=pending`,
          { headers: { Authorization: 'Bearer test-token' } }
        );
        const data = await response.json();
        expect(data[0]).toHaveProperty('startDate');
        expect(data[0]).toHaveProperty('status', 'pending');
      });
  });
});

// Provider verification (Fastify side)
import { Verifier } from '@pact-foundation/pact';

new Verifier({
  providerBaseUrl: 'http://localhost:3000',
  pactBrokerUrl: process.env.PACT_BROKER_URL,
  provider: 'HRLeaveAPI',
  stateHandlers: {
    'there are pending leaves': async () => {
      await prisma.leave.createMany({
        data: [createMockLeave({ status: 'pending' })],
      });
    },
  },
}).verifyProvider();

5. Debugging Narrative from Manoj's Experience

A backend developer renamed the user field to employee in the leave response, breaking the Angular dashboard (which expected leave.user.name). Without contract tests, this was only caught 2 hours later when QA tested the deployed version. With Pact contracts, the provider verification would have failed in CI: "Expected response to contain field 'user' but got 'employee'." The deployment would have been blocked. Manoj set up a Pact Broker and the can-i-deploy CI gate — the backend PR couldn't merge until the Angular consumer updated its contract to accept employee, and the backend verified against the new contract.

6. Pitfall & Mitigation Table

PitfallImpact at ScaleMitigation
No contract tests between frontend/backendBreaking changes discovered in productionPact consumer-driven contracts in CI
Contracts not versionedOld consumer contracts block new featuresPact Broker with version tags + can-i-deploy
Provider doesn't run verificationConsumer contracts are defined but never validatedProvider verification as mandatory CI step
Too strict matchersEvery minor response change breaks contractsUse flexible matchers (eachLike, string, integer) not exact values
No state setup in providerVerification fails because test data is missingstateHandlers seed required data before verification

7. Validation Strategy

  • CI gate: pact-broker can-i-deploy must pass before any service deploys.
  • Consumer coverage: Every API endpoint consumed by Angular has a corresponding Pact test.
  • Provider verification: Runs on every backend PR; fail blocks merge.
  • Breaking change alert: Pact Broker webhook notifies Slack when a provider verification fails.

1. Definition & Core Concept

Contract Test performance bottlenecks appear as the number of consumers and interactions grows: slow Pact Broker lookups, large contract files slowing CI, provider verification needing to set up state for hundreds of interactions, and cross-team coordination overhead for contract changes. Instrumenting requires measuring contract verification time, broker latency, and the time spent on state setup.

2. Concrete Use-Case from Manoj's Projects

In the HR Leave Management System, the contract suite grew to 30 interactions (12 endpoints × 2-3 scenarios each). Provider verification took 2 minutes because each interaction required database seeding via stateHandlers. Manoj optimized: (1) Shared state setup: instead of seeding per interaction, seed once in beforeAll with data covering all scenarios (users, leaves at various statuses, approvals at all levels). (2) Parallel verification: group interactions by resource and verify in parallel. (3) Cached broker: use pact-broker with local cache to avoid re-downloading unchanged contracts.

3. Visual SVG Diagram

4. Code Example

// Optimized provider verification with shared state
import { Verifier } from '@pact-foundation/pact';

// Seed ONCE with data covering all interaction scenarios
async function seedAllScenarios(prisma: PrismaClient) {
  const user = await prisma.user.create({
    data: { name: 'Manoj', email: 'manoj@hr.com', role: 'employee' },
  });
  const manager = await prisma.user.create({
    data: { name: 'Manager', email: 'mgr@hr.com', role: 'team_lead' },
  });
  await prisma.leaveBalance.create({
    data: { userId: user.id, total: 24, remaining: 20 },
  });
  // Leaves at various statuses for all interactions
  await prisma.leave.createMany({
    data: [
      createMockLeave({ userId: user.id, status: 'pending' }),
      createMockLeave({ userId: user.id, status: 'approved' }),
      createMockLeave({ userId: user.id, status: 'rejected' }),
    ],
  });
  await prisma.approval.create({
    data: { leaveId: 'leave-approved', managerId: manager.id, level: 1 },
  });
}

// Provider verification — shared state, no per-interaction seeding
beforeAll(async () => {
  await seedAllScenarios(prisma);
});

const verifier = new Verifier({
  providerBaseUrl: 'http://localhost:3000',
  pactBrokerUrl: process.env.PACT_BROKER_URL,
  provider: 'HRLeaveAPI',
  publishVerificationResult: process.env.CI === 'true',
  providerVersion: process.env.GIT_SHA,
  // No individual stateHandlers — all data pre-seeded
  stateHandlers: {
    'there are pending leaves': async () => { /* already seeded */ },
    'there are approved leaves': async () => { /* already seeded */ },
    'user has leave balance': async () => { /* already seeded */ },
  },
});

await verifier.verifyProvider();

5. Debugging Narrative from Manoj's Experience

Contract verification CI time crept from 30s to 2 minutes as Manoj added more interactions. Profiling showed that each stateHandler ran a full prisma.user.create() + prisma.leave.create() sequence — 30 interactions × 4 DB calls each = 120 DB operations, mostly redundant (the same user was created 30 times). Refactoring to a single seedAllScenarios() function that created all needed data once reduced DB operations from 120 to 8. Verification time dropped from 2 minutes to 15 seconds. The key insight: make state handlers idempotent and share the base dataset.

6. Pitfall & Mitigation Table

PitfallImpact at ScaleMitigation
Individual state setup per interactionRedundant DB seeding, slow verificationShared beforeAll seed covering all scenarios
Large Pact contract filesSlow broker upload/downloadCompress contracts; use Pact Broker with caching
Verification blocks on broker availabilityBroker outage blocks all deploymentsLocal fallback: cache latest contracts in repo
Cross-team coordination for contract changesDays of back-and-forth on contract updatesAuto-generated contracts from TypeScript types; PR-based reviews
No versioning of contractsOld consumers block provider changes indefinitelyTag contracts with consumer version; deprecation policy

7. Validation Strategy

  • Verification time: Contract verification < 30s; alert if exceeded.
  • State coverage: Every interaction has its scenario seeded in beforeAll; no empty stateHandlers.
  • Broker health: Monitor Pact Broker availability; local fallback if down for > 5 minutes.
  • Version compatibility: can-i-deploy matrix shows all consumer-provider version pairs.
